You are free: to copy, distribute, display, and perform the work, and to make derivative works under the following conditions: you must attribute the work in the manner specified by the licensor; you may not use this work for commercial purposes; if you alter, transform, or build upon this work, you may distribute the resulting work only under a license identical to this one. For any reuse or distribution, you must make clear to others the license terms of this work. Any of these conditions can be waived if you get permission from the licensor. Your fair use and other rights are in no way affected by the above.

div.head: LECTURE XII.

GENESIS, XXIV. 4.
But thou shalt go unto my own country and to my kindred, and take a wife unto my son Isaac.
div.p:
It is characteristic of the Holy Scriptures, and the observation applies as closely and correctly as the Old as to the New Testament, that its most ordinary narratives, those parts which do not seem to be immediately connected with any important precept or sublime doctrine, excite an attention, and fill the mind with an interest, superior to that which any other hsitory, ancient or modern, can produce. Need we seek wide for the solution of this problem? There can be but one. The Holy Spirit of God has, in all things, ruled and directed those who have been appointed to record the seperate history of God's chosen people. Moses, and those who after him discharaged the office of national historians, did not write as all Gentile historians have written, in the vanity of their mind, supplying the chasms of their ignorance by presumptuous assertionas-
sertion or incautious conjecture, often avowedly with a design to illustrate individuals, and almost always influenced by an unjust preference of their own family, tribe, or kingdom. But those who we call sacred historians could not write otherwise than the truth. Hence is diffused that charm over every page which recounts even the domestic occurences of the patriarchs. Nor can there be any stronger internal evidence of the truth of the Scriptures required or offered, than the zeal with which they are perused, the facility with which our memory recalls their principal contents, and the clearness with which every description strikes our sense. Those who can read nothing else with taste, can always read the Bible; and some, after passing a long life of erudition, after a high cultivation of their taste, by culling the choicest productions of human literature, have finally abandoned all other books, and found in their Bible the one thing delightful, as well as needful. The hearts of those who are happily conversant with the holy volume, bear testimony to the truth of what I say; and those who have not yet only a partial acquaintance with it, may learn, with confidence in such testimony, that their highest mental delight is yet reserved for them in the knowledge of God's holy word, and his ways of old, What, indeed, can be so fit for our perusal, as the history of the human heart,
developed by the prophets of Him unto whom all hearts are open? Whatever, therefore, has been revealed to us at all connected with God's promises to Abraham, let us by no means call unimportant; rather consider all things profitable unto godliness, whatever the Scriptures have set before us, lest in the pride of worldly wisdom we quency the Holy Spirit.

The general purport of the chapter from which I have taken my text, is to mark the vigilance of Abraham, in conforming his own conduct to the will of God, and his zeal in saving his son Isaac from all those temptations which were most likely to draw him from his duty, and occasion thereby a forfeiture of God's promises.

Abraham having lost his wife Sarah, and being much advanced in eyars, at the age of one hundred and forty, grateful to God for the favours showered down on him during his life, turned his thoughts, like an obedient servant, to the fulfilment of his final duties on earth, namely, of providing for the moral and religious conduct of his posterity. Abraham is, in this respect, a model for the observance and imitation of parents to the latest times. Though they may be very easily too anxious about the worldly prosperity of their children, yet they can never labour too much to set up the law of righteousnessrighteous-
ness in the hearts of those who come after: they cannot make too ample provision of that meat which perishes not. Neither did Abraham defer this truly parental and pious work until too late an hour. He suffered not the decrepitude of age, nor the pains of final disease, to render his faculties imperfect. He set his house in order, while it was yet day; and he provided for the contingencies of the future, while the present was in his power. This i salso a lesson of no small import, as the too frequent neglect of such duties proves almost at every moment. It is very criminal, as well as weak, to suppose that we have a right to leave the settlement of our worldly affairs for the occupation of our last moments: we can never know what moments will be our last; and if we did, we should never spare one of them for that strict communion with God, that intense meditation on approaching judgment, which casts a veil over every earthly thought. He who is faithful over a few things, gives the most confident assurance, that he will be faithful over many things. The deliberate and rational settlement of our worldly affairs, whilst we are in full possession of health and strength, besides other recommendations, has this: it leads our thoughts in good time to a settlement of a much more awful account; it is an operation which must check the presumptionpresump-
tion of youth and vigour, which are apt to whisper that we shall live long and not see the grave.

Abraham's chief anxiety concerning Isaac was, that he should not take a wife unto him from the daughters of the land; from the Canaanites, the descendants of Noah's second son whose conduct had drawn down on him a severer curse than any which had been previously pronounced by God, except on Cain. The Canaanites were at the time, in general, idolaters, and given over to all those brutual, gross, and revolting vices, which have ever been msyteriously connected with false religion. The Canaanites were even then marked our for destruction; their fruitful territories were even then promised inheritance of Abraham's posterity; nothing, therefore, on his part, could have been so absurd, nothing so contradictory to the avowed intentions of Providence, as to have engrafted his own family on that of any other, which was destined to be swept away by the sword of the Lord. Where was it prudent, where natural, where pious for him to seek her who was to be the mother of Israel? In the land of his own ancestry, in a countyr where he himself had been brought up in the knowledge of the true God.
